summary: Gruber critiques Humane's wearable AI device, arguing its entire premise is fundamentally flawed because it positions itself as liberating people from their phones — but people don't want to be liberated from their phones. He draws a parallel to Microsoft's failed Windows Phone 7 campaign, which similarly tried to sell people on using their phones less. Gruber argues that fans filming events on their phones aren't burdened but delighted, and that personal photos serve as proof of attendance rather than artistic endeavors. He contends that successful accessories like Apple Watch and AirPods thrive as iPhone satellites, not replacements, and that any post-phone device must match or exceed what phones offer rather than simply remove the screen.
key_points: Humane's anti-phone marketing premise is fundamentally flawed because people love their phones and don't feel burdened by them
People photograph live events not for photo quality but as proof of attendance — selfies are the new autographs
Microsoft made the identical mistake with Windows Phone 7's 'use your phone less' ad campaign, and it failed
Apple Watch and AirPods succeed precisely because they complement the iPhone rather than trying to replace it
A post-phone device must do everything phones do or offer compelling new experiences — a chest-mounted laser projector does neither
money_quotes: "Finally, you can replace this thing that you despise" is a powerful marketing message. "Finally, you can replace this thing that you love" is not.
The fundamental flaw in Humane's entire premise, as I see it, is that people don't feel burdened by their phones. People love them — especially iPhone owners.
Selfies are the new autographs, and a shaky iPhone photo from the perspective of your own seat at the event is the new certificate of attendance.
The problem is that people stare at their phones all the time not because they have to but because they want to.
Apple Watch and AirPods thrive because they're satellites to our iPhones, not ostensible replacements. Our iPhones aren't a burden — they're a pleasure.
Anything that attempts to establish a post-phone beachhead has to do the things we love to do with our phones, or entertain us in new ways that make us forget about them.
The notion of even a single trip to the grocery store without a phone to provide (unlimited!) material to read or watch while I wait at the checkout line seems like veritable torture to me now.
key_insight: Any device positioned as a phone replacement is doomed to fail because its premise misdiagnoses attachment to phones as a burden rather than a pleasure.
